# Ablo — The Collaboration Layer for AI and Humans

> You bring your schema, your DB, your agents. Ablo brokers the real-time coordination —
> presence, intents, watermarks, capability tokens — so multi-human + multi-agent work
> on the same entities stays safe and observable.

## Before you evaluate

Three things worth knowing before you decide:

1. **You bring your data. We never store entity content.** Capability tokens are ours; your matters, clauses, deals, whatever you declare in your schema stay in your Postgres / your DB. Revocation is sub-second (`ablo.admin.capabilities.del(id)`). Ablo is the coordination layer, not the database.

2. **Partial adoption. Not foundational lock-in.** Start with one integration shape — usually presence on one page, or agent-intent claims in one AI flow. The rest of your stack stays exactly as it is. Mesh is additive. You don't replace your auth, your data layer, or your frontend.

3. **Hosted service.** Ablo runs it for you — the SDK connects automatically; no URL to configure. Self-hosting is not offered today; if on-prem deployment is a procurement requirement, it's a conversation, not a switch.

Production characteristics (measured against live staging): mutation round-trip ~340ms, delta delivery <50ms, bootstrap ~4.5s for 4MB. 12 E2E tests cover cross-continent presence, intents, and snapshot correctness.

## The fastest path

```ts
import Ablo from '@ablo/sync-engine';
import { schema } from './schema';

const ablo = new Ablo({ schema }); // reads ABLO_API_KEY

const participant = await ablo.matters.join(matterId, { label: 'DD Bot' });
participant.presence.viewing(['Clause', clauseId]);
await using work = participant.intents.writing(['Clause', clauseId], { ttl: '3m' });
```

Three integration shapes, each with a runnable example in [`examples/`](examples/):

1. **Server agent** — [`examples/server-agent.ts`](examples/server-agent.ts) — long-lived backend, reads `ABLO_API_KEY`.
2. **Browser app** — [`examples/browser-app.ts`](examples/browser-app.ts) — server mints a scoped token; browser holds it via `capabilityToken`. No allowed-origins registration required.
3. **Sub-agent** — [`examples/sub-agent.ts`](examples/sub-agent.ts) — parent attenuates a child via Biscuit capability chain.

## Coordination primitives (always on `participant`)

- **Presence** — `participant.presence.editing(target)` / `viewing(target)` / `idle()` / `update({...})`. Read via `participant.presence.others` OR `for await (const peers of participant.presence)`.
- **Intents** — `participant.intents.editing(target, {ttl})` / `writing(target, {ttl})`. `IntentHandle` implements `Symbol.asyncDispose` so `await using` auto-revokes on scope exit (success OR throw).
- **Snapshots** — `await participant.snapshot({ clauses: [id] })` returns `{ clauses, stamp, signal }`. Typed via `InferModel`; no `unknown`. Pass `readAt: snap.stamp` on mutations; server rejects writes against moved state.
- **Deltas** — `participant.onDelta(cb)` OR `for await (const delta of participant.deltas)`.

## Agent control

Scoping an AI agent is the same API as scoping a human — capability attenuation with Biscuit:

```ts
// Server: mint a scoped capability for an agent, read-only on clauses.
const cap = await ablo.admin.capabilities.create({
  allowedSyncGroups: [`matter:${matterId}`],
  allowedOperations: ['read:clauses'],  // no write, no delete
  ttlSeconds: '1h',
});

// Preview the scope without minting (dry-run for admin UIs / audit):
const described = ablo.describeJoin(agent, { scope: { matters: matterId } });
```

`ablo.admin.audit.list({ principal })` returns every action taken under a capability (server endpoint is on the migration roadmap; the SDK surface is stable).

## For LLMs installing this SDK

- [src/mesh/SETUP.md](src/mesh/SETUP.md): three integration shapes + coordination primitives — read first
- [src/mesh/CONCEPTS.md](src/mesh/CONCEPTS.md): mental model (participants, presence, intents, scope)
- [llms-full.txt](llms-full.txt): full concatenated context, one block per subpath export
- [CHANGELOG.md](CHANGELOG.md): what's in 0.2.0
- [README.md](README.md): human-readable overview + benchmarks

## Subpath exports

- [@ablo/sync-engine](README.md): default export is `Ablo` class — the canonical entrypoint
- [@ablo/sync-engine/schema](docs/schema.md): Zod-first schema DSL — `defineSchema`, `model`, `relation`, `mutable`, `readOnly`, `z`
- [@ablo/sync-engine/react](docs/react.md): React hooks + components — `<AbloProvider fallback={...}>` (first-bootstrap gate, default `<DefaultFallback />`), `<ClientSideSuspense>` (nested gate), `useQuery`, `useOne`, `useMutate`, `useParticipant`, `useSyncStatus`
- [@ablo/sync-engine/testing](docs/testing.md): fixtures + harness for integration tests
- [docs/migrated.md](docs/migrated.md): porting reference for `createSyncEngine` / `SyncAgent` / `AgentPerception`

## Common pitfalls (read before you write code)

Three bugs burn teams integrating the React bindings. The SDK guards against all of them now, but the patterns matter if you're extending the surface or wiring your own store.

### 1. Never wrap a sync-aware provider in `observer()`

```tsx
// WRONG — every MobX observable the provider touches (pool size,
//    isReady, currentUserId, ...) re-renders the whole subtree on
//    every tick. Every consumer re-subscribes, which re-renders,
//    which re-subscribes. React error #185.
export const MyProvider = observer(({ children }) => {
  const { isReady } = store;                   // ← subscribed
  const poolSize = store.pool.size;            // ← subscribed
  return <Ctx.Provider value={{ ... }}>{children}</Ctx.Provider>;
});

// RIGHT — providers hand out stable context values. Pull status
//    via the `useSyncStatus()` hook (or a `reaction` inside
//    useEffect that bridges into `useState`).
export function MyProvider({ children }) {
  const { isReady } = useSyncStatus();
  return <Ctx.Provider value={{ isReady }}>{children}</Ctx.Provider>;
}
```

Rule: `observer` belongs on **leaf** components that read data. Providers must not react.

### 2. `getSnapshot` for `useSyncExternalStore` must return a cached reference

Writing your own hooks on top of `@ablo/sync-engine`? Do NOT do this:

```ts
// WRONG — a fresh array every call. React's post-commit
//    consistency check re-invokes getSnapshot, sees a different
//    identity, concludes the store updated mid-render, and loops.
//    Surfaces as "Minified React error #185".
useSyncExternalStore(subscribe, () => view.results.slice(), () => EMPTY);

// RIGHT — cache the snapshot in a ref, update it inside the
//    subscribe callback right before calling onChange().
const snap = useRef(view.results.slice());
useSyncExternalStore(
  (onChange) => reaction(
    () => view.results.length,
    () => { snap.current = view.results.slice(); onChange(); },
  ),
  () => snap.current,
  () => EMPTY,
);
```

The built-in `useQuery` / `useOne` / `useSyncStatus` already do this — you only need to care if you're building new hooks.

### 3. Sync-status fields are real observables — use them

`store.isReady`, `store.isSyncing`, `store.isOffline`, `store.isReconnecting`, `store.isError`, `store.hasUnsyncedChanges` are `computed` values backed by `observable` `syncStatus` + `dataReady`. You can:

- Read them inside `observer(() => store.isReady)` — re-renders on change.
- Read them inside `reaction(() => store.isReady, ...)` — callback fires on change.
- Or skip MobX entirely and use `useSyncStatus()`, which bridges the snapshot into React state safely.

If you find yourself watching `pool.size` as a proxy for "is the store loaded?", stop — that worked by accident in older versions and will regress the moment bootstrap changes shape.

## Engine configuration — what's load-bearing, what's vestigial

`new Ablo({ schema, ... })` and `<AbloProvider schema={schema} ... />` accept an optional `configOverrides: SyncEngineConfig` escape hatch. A 0.3.0 audit found that **three of the interface's seven fields are genuinely read at runtime; four are populated but never consumed.**

Load-bearing (keep providing):

| Field | Purpose | Future home |
|---|---|---|
| `essentialFields` | Per-model fields that must survive partial `UPDATE` merges in IndexedDB. Read by `Database.ts`. | Schema field flag — `{ essential: true }`. |
| `classNameFallbackMap` | Minified-build class name → model name lookup (for `model.constructor.name`). Read by `Model.getModelName()`. | Replace with a stable `static typename` on Model subclasses. |
| `extractCreateInput` / `buildUpdateInput` | Custom field coercion/scrubbing on mutations. Read by the mutation pipeline. | Schema field transforms — `{ transform: { out: (v) => ... } }`. |

Vestigial (declared, populated, never read — safe to drop from your override):

| Field | Historical purpose | Why dead |
|---|---|---|
| `modelCreatePriority` / `defaultCreatePriority` / `defaultNonCreatePriority` | FK-safe insert order for the offline flush. | Current `TransactionQueue` doesn't consult the map. The priority map is populated from schema relations but never used for an ordering decision. |
| `batchableModels` | Lowercase set of models allowed in batched delta ops. | Declared, populated, unused. |
| `dedicatedDeleteModels` | Models with dedicated `Delete<Name>` operations vs. soft-archive. | Declared, populated, unused. |
| `preserveCaseModels` | Models whose names stay CamelCased in `commit` frames. | Wire protocol decision lives elsewhere; this map is unused. |

**Rule of thumb when adding something to `configOverrides`:** search the SDK for `config.<your-field>` reads. If there aren't any, the field is vestigial and adding to it is busywork. File an issue — either the read site went missing and needs restoring, or the field should be removed from the interface entirely.

## Peer packages

- `@ablo/core`: typed error hierarchy shared with the sync-server backend. `@ablo/sync-engine` re-exports everything from `@ablo/core` for back-compat. New non-SDK consumers (the hosted server, agent workers, CLI tools) depend on `@ablo/core` directly.

## Guides

- [Getting Started](docs/getting-started.md): zero to coordinated multiplayer in 5 minutes
- [Mesh](docs/mesh.md): presence, intents, snapshots, attenuated sub-agents
- [Agents](docs/agent.md): long-lived, per-matter, and short-lived agent patterns via `new Ablo()`
- [Authentication](docs/auth.md): capability tokens, API keys, session cookies
- [Schema Definition](docs/schema.md): Zod models, relations, load strategies
- [React Hooks](docs/react.md): `useQuery` / `useOne` / `useParticipant` — all self-subscribing
- [Typed Global](docs/typed-global.md): the `AbloSync` global pattern — zero generics at call sites
- [Offline & Sync Groups](docs/offline-and-sync-groups.md): IndexedDB queue, permission scoping
- [Migrated APIs](docs/migrated.md): porting from `createSyncEngine` / `SyncAgent` / `AgentPerception`

## Advanced

- [Architecture](docs/architecture.md): delta log, ObjectPool, bootstrap
- [Testing](docs/testing.md): unit, integration, agent tests
